schools.summary: Gill: Jacob called his sons, who were near at hand or whom he sent for, to tell them what should befall them in the latter days; the witnesses note Jacob calling his sons together to tell them what should befall them in the last days — the solemn opening of the patriarch's prophetic blessing, the dying father gathering his sons to declare their future (Jacob, near death, summoning his sons for a prophetic word: 'Gather yourselves together, that I may tell you that which shall befall you in the last days'; the gathering, that Jacob 'called unto his sons' and bade them 'gather... together,' that all the twelve might hear at once the words he was about to speak; the prophetic character of what follows, 'that I may tell you that which shall befall you in the last days,' that Jacob, by the Spirit of God, would foretell the future of each son and his tribe, even to 'the last days,' reaching down through the generations and, in Judah's portion, to the days of Messiah; that this was no mere fatherly counsel but a prophecy, the aged patriarch made a prophet in his dying hour; the solemnity of a deathbed blessing, the last and weightiest words of the father of the tribes).

themes: And Jacob called unto his sons, and said, Gather yourselves together
that I may tell you that which shall befall you in the last days
the solemn opening of the patriarch's prophetic blessing
the dying patriarch made a prophet, reaching to the days of Messiah

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the repeated summons, Gather yourselves together and hear, ye sons of Jacob, and hearken unto Israel your father; Gill: this is repeated to hasten them and to suggest he had something of importance to make known, which he chose to do when they were together; the witnesses note Jacob repeating the call to gather and hear, as sons of Jacob, and to hearken to Israel their father — the urgent and twofold summons to attend, marking the weight of the coming words (Jacob doubling his call to fix their attention: 'Gather yourselves together, and hear, ye sons of Jacob; and hearken unto Israel your father'; the repetition, that Jacob says 'gather yourselves together' again, to hasten them and to mark that he had a weighty thing to declare, fit to be heard by all at once; the double word for attention, 'hear... and hearken,' pressing them to give earnest heed to what their dying father should say; the two names the commentators mark, 'ye sons of Jacob' and 'Israel your father,' the natural name and the covenant name, that they should hear both as the children of Jacob and as the heirs of the Israel of God, to whom the promises belonged; the solemnity of the charge, fitting words that were prophecy and last testament both).

themes: Gather yourselves together, and hear, ye sons of Jacob
and hearken unto Israel your father
Jacob's urgent twofold summons to earnest attention
naming them both sons of Jacob and heirs of Israel, the covenant name

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob addressed Reuben first, owned him as his firstborn, did not cashier him from his family nor disinherit him, but acknowledged his natural pre-eminence, the beginning of his strength, to whom belonged the dignity and power of the firstborn; the witnesses note Jacob owning Reuben as his firstborn, his might and the beginning of his strength, the excellency of dignity and of power — the acknowledgment of Reuben's natural birthright dignity before the sentence that follows (Jacob first setting out what Reuben was by birth: 'Reuben, thou art my firstborn, my might, and the beginning of my strength, the excellency of dignity, and the excellency of power'; the honour owned, that Reuben was Jacob's 'firstborn,' 'the beginning of my strength,' the first fruit of his manhood, to whom by birth belonged 'the excellency of dignity, and the excellency of power,' the firstborn's threefold prerogative of the double portion, the priesthood, and the rule in the family; that Jacob does not deny Reuben his natural place as eldest, owning him fully and tenderly; that this acknowledgment makes the coming sentence the more solemn, that one born to such dignity should forfeit it; the setting-up of the height from which Reuben fell).

themes: Reuben, thou art my firstborn, my might, and the beginning of my strength
the excellency of dignity, and the excellency of power
Jacob owning Reuben's natural birthright dignity
the firstborn's threefold prerogative, the height from which he fell

schools.summary: Gill: this is not to be understood of mere levity of mind but of the impetuous force of his lust breaking forth like water, carrying him into sin; Reuben should not excel, his tribe never rising to greatness; the witnesses note Jacob declaring Reuben unstable as water and that he should not excel, because he defiled his father's bed — the solemn sentence of forfeited pre-eminence for Reuben's grievous sin (Jacob pronouncing the loss of the birthright: 'Unstable as water, thou shalt not excel; because thou wentest up to thy father's bed; then defiledst thou it'; the figure, 'Unstable as water,' that Reuben was like water, which boils up and overflows but has no firmness, his passions ungoverned, breaking forth in the impetuous lust that carried him into sin; the sentence, 'thou shalt not excel,' that, for all his birthright dignity, Reuben should not have the pre-eminence, his tribe never rising to greatness or rule in Israel; the cause, 'because thou wentest up to thy father's bed; then defiledst thou it,' the grievous sin of Reuben with Bilhah, by which he dishonoured his father and forfeited his place; the justice of God, that sin loses what nature gave, the firstborn's honour passing from Reuben, the rule to Judah, the priesthood to Levi, the double portion to Joseph).

debates.question: Why does Reuben forfeit the birthright?

sides.position: For his grievous sin in defiling his father's bed with Bilhah (Genesis 35:22) — his ungoverned passion, 'unstable as water,' losing the firstborn's pre-eminence; the honour passing to Judah (rule), Levi (priesthood), and Joseph (double portion)

themes: Unstable as water, thou shalt not excel
because thou wentest up to thy father's bed; then defiledst thou it
the sentence on Reuben, his ungoverned passion forfeiting the birthright
the justice of God, that sin loses what nature gave

schools.summary: Gill: they are called brethren not merely as being so by father and mother, for so were others, but because they were of like tempers, dispositions, and conduct, alike in their fierce and cruel disposition; the witnesses note Jacob calling Simeon and Levi brethren, with instruments of cruelty in their habitations — the joining of the two for their shared fierce and cruel temper, shown at Shechem (Jacob marking them as one in their violence: 'Simeon and Levi are brethren; instruments of cruelty are in their habitations'; the sense of 'brethren' here, not the natural tie only, for all twelve were brethren, but that Simeon and Levi were 'brethren' in temper, alike in their fierce, cruel, and revengeful disposition, confederate in evil; the charge, 'instruments of cruelty are in their habitations,' that their swords, or their counsels and devices, were instruments of cruelty, pointing back to the bloody deed at Shechem, where they slew all the males in revenge for their sister Dinah; that Jacob, even on his deathbed, would not pass over so great a sin but marks it for the warning of their tribes; the difference of this from a blessing, that for these two the father's word was reproof and sentence).

themes: Simeon and Levi are brethren
instruments of cruelty are in their habitations
Jacob joining Simeon and Levi as brethren in fierce, cruel temper
their swords instruments of cruelty, pointing to the bloody deed at Shechem

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob said this as abhorring the wicked counsel they had taken of slaying the Shechemites, and lest any should think he had a hand in it; the witnesses note Jacob praying that his soul come not into their secret nor his honour be joined to their assembly, for in their anger they slew a man and in their selfwill digged down a wall — the patriarch's solemn disavowal of their bloody deed, clearing himself of all share in it (Jacob abhorring and renouncing their counsel: 'O my soul, come not thou into their secret... for in their anger they slew a man, and in their selfwill they digged down a wall'; the earnest disclaiming, 'O my soul, come not thou into their secret; unto their assembly, mine honour, be not thou united,' that Jacob, abhorring their 'secret,' their cruel counsel and conspiracy at Shechem, prays that he had no part in it, neither his 'soul' nor his 'honour' joined to so bloody a deed; the charge stated plainly, 'in their anger they slew a man,' the men of Shechem, 'and in their selfwill they digged down a wall,' or houghed oxen, broke down the city, a deed of fierce anger and stubborn self-will; that Jacob, far from owning the deed, here solemnly clears himself before God and his sons of any share in their cruelty; the abhorrence of a godly man for bloody violence done even in a kinsman's cause).

themes: O my soul, come not thou into their secret; unto their assembly, mine honour, be not thou united
for in their anger they slew a man, and in their selfwill they digged down a wall
Jacob's solemn disavowal of the bloody deed at Shechem
the abhorrence of a godly man for fierce violence in a kinsman's cause

schools.summary: Gill: their anger was sinful in its nature, criminal and detestable, strong, fierce, and furious in its operation, and so justly cursed, not the persons but the sin; the witnesses note Jacob cursing their fierce anger and cruel wrath, and declaring he would divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel — the sentence on their sin and its strange fulfilment in the scattering of the two tribes (Jacob cursing the sin and foretelling the punishment: 'Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce; and their wrath, for it was cruel: I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel'; the careful aim of the curse, 'Cursed be their anger... and their wrath,' that Jacob curses not the persons of his sons but their 'anger' and 'wrath,' the sinful passion itself, 'fierce' and 'cruel,' justly accursed; the sentence, 'I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel,' that the two tribes should be scattered and not have a settled, undivided inheritance of their own; the remarkable fulfilment the commentators trace, that Simeon's portion lay scattered within Judah's border and the tribe dwindled, while Levi received no land at all but was dispersed in cities throughout Israel, yet Levi's scattering, by God's grace, was turned to honour in the priesthood; the truth that God can make even a curse the means of mercy, as in Levi).

debates.question: How was the scattering of Simeon and Levi fulfilled?

sides.position: Simeon's portion lay scattered within Judah's border and the tribe dwindled; Levi received no land but was dispersed in cities throughout Israel — yet Levi's scattering, by God's grace, was turned to honour in the priesthood

themes: Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce; and their wrath, for it was cruel
I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel
Jacob cursing the sin, not the persons, and foretelling the scattering
fulfilled in Simeon's dwindling and Levi's dispersal, yet Levi's turned to honour

schools.summary: Gill: Judah's name signifies praise, given by his mother whose heart was filled with praises to God; he should be praised by his brethren for his virtues, his tribe famous for heroes such as David, and especially his famous seed the Messiah, of whom he was a type; the witnesses note Judah being praised by his brethren, his hand on the neck of his enemies, and his brethren bowing before him — the turn from sentence to blessing, the pre-eminence given to Judah and pointing to Christ (Jacob exalting Judah above his brethren: 'Judah, thou art he whom thy brethren shall praise: thy hand shall be in the neck of thine enemies; thy father's children shall bow down before thee'; the play on the name, 'Judah' meaning 'praise,' that he should be 'he whom thy brethren shall praise,' honoured and esteemed for his virtues; the victory promised, 'thy hand shall be in the neck of thine enemies,' that Judah should subdue his foes in conquest; the rule given, 'thy father's children shall bow down before thee,' that the other tribes should yield him pre-eminence and homage; the great type the commentators mark, that all this points beyond the tribe to Christ, the Lion of Judah, whom his brethren praise, who subdues his enemies and before whom every knee shall bow).

debates.question: Whom does the exaltation of Judah ultimately point to?

sides.position: Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah — whom his brethren praise, who subdues his enemies, and before whom every knee shall bow; Judah and his pre-eminence are a type of the Messiah who springs from his line

themes: Judah, thou art he whom thy brethren shall praise; thy hand shall be in the neck of thine enemies
thy father's children shall bow down before thee
Judah exalted above his brethren, praised, victorious, given rule
a type of Christ, the Lion of Judah, before whom every knee shall bow

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Jacob compares Judah to a young lion, gone up from the prey, lying down like a lion, like a lioness whom none can rouse; Gill: he was comparable to a young lion for his strength, courage, and generosity; the witnesses note Judah likened to a lion's whelp, gone up from the prey, couched as a lion whom none dare rouse — the figure of the lion that marks Judah's strength and dominion, and points to Christ the Lion of Judah (Jacob setting forth Judah's lion-like majesty: 'Judah is a lion's whelp: from the prey, my son, thou art gone up: he stooped down, he couched as a lion... who shall rouse him up?'; the threefold image, the 'lion's whelp,' the young lion growing in strength, the lion 'gone up from the prey,' returned victorious from the hunt, and the lion that has 'couched' or 'lain down' in such majesty and might that none dare 'rouse him up'; that this figures Judah's growth from a young tribe to a strong and ruling one, courageous, victorious, and secure in his strength; the great Messianic reference the commentators draw, that the 'Lion of the tribe of Judah' is Christ himself, strong to conquer, who, having gone up from the prey of his finished work, reigns in unassailable majesty; the dignity of the figure, fit for both the tribe and its great Son).

debates.question: What does the lion figure of Judah point to?

sides.position: Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah (Revelation 5:5) — strong to conquer, victorious from the prey of his finished work, reigning in unassailable majesty; the lion figures both the tribe's strength and dominion and its great Son

themes: Judah is a lion's whelp: from the prey, my son, thou art gone up
he couched as a lion, and as an old lion; who shall rouse him up?
Judah the lion, figuring strength and dominion
pointing to the Lion of the tribe of Judah, Christ reigning in majesty

schools.summary: Gill: the sceptre signifies dominion, power, and authority, not the bare continuance of the tribe, for Benjamin and Levi also continued; it should not depart from Judah until Shiloh come, that is, the Messiah, to whom the gathering of the people should be; the witnesses note Jacob foretelling that the sceptre and lawgiver should not depart from Judah until Shiloh come, and unto him the gathering of the people — the supreme Messianic prophecy of the chapter, the rule abiding in Judah until Christ (Jacob speaking the great word of promise: 'The sceptre shall not depart from Judah, nor a lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh come; and unto him shall the gathering of the people be'; the meaning of 'the sceptre' and 'lawgiver,' not the bare continuance of the tribe but 'dominion, power, and authority,' that rule and government should belong to Judah and not depart until a set time; the great term 'Shiloh,' which the commentators take for the Messiah, whether read as 'he whose right it is,' 'the peaceable one,' or 'the sent,' that the sceptre should abide in Judah 'until Shiloh come,' until the coming of Christ who sprang from Judah; the fulfilment, that Judah held a distinct tribal and ruling standing until the time of Christ, and that with his coming the old polity passed; the gathering, 'unto him shall the gathering of the people be,' that to Shiloh the nations should be gathered, the Gentiles brought in).

debates.question: Who is 'Shiloh,' and what is the meaning of the prophecy?

sides.position: The Messiah, Christ, who sprang from Judah — the commentators read 'Shiloh' as 'he whose right it is,' 'the peaceable one,' or 'the sent'; dominion abides in Judah until his coming, and to him ('the gathering of the people') the nations and Gentiles are gathered

themes: The sceptre shall not depart from Judah, nor a lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh come
and unto him shall the gathering of the people be
the supreme Messianic prophecy, rule abiding in Judah until Shiloh the Messiah
to him the gathering of the people, the Gentiles brought in

schools.summary: Gill: this may signify that vines should grow in such plenty in Judah's land that a man might bind his ass to a vine, Judah's land as Engedi being famous for vines, or it may be applied to Shiloh the Messiah; the witnesses note the binding of the foal to the vine and the washing of garments in wine and the blood of grapes — the figures of the rich plenty of Judah's land, applied also to Christ and his blood (Jacob painting abundance under the vine and the wine: 'Binding his foal unto the vine, and his ass's colt unto the choice vine; he washed his garments in wine, and his clothes in the blood of grapes'; the first figure, 'binding his foal unto the vine... unto the choice vine,' that Judah's land should so abound in vines, large and strong, that one might tie an ass to a vine as to a post and not grieve if it ate of so plentiful a growth, a picture of great fruitfulness; the second figure, 'he washed his garments in wine, and his clothes in the blood of grapes,' the grapes so plentiful that wine should be as common as water for washing, a hyperbole of abundance; the Messianic application the commentators draw, that 'the blood of grapes' points to the blood of Christ, the true Vine, who treads the winepress and washes his garments in blood; the richness fit for both the fruitful tribe and its great Son).

themes: Binding his foal unto the vine, and his ass's colt unto the choice vine
he washed his garments in wine, and his clothes in the blood of grapes
the figures of Judah's rich plenty, vines and wine in abundance
the Messianic glance, the blood of grapes pointing to the blood of Christ, the true Vine

schools.summary: Gill: this signifies not the intemperance of the tribe but the goodness and generosity of their wine, that Judah's land should abound in wine and milk, and (applied to Messiah) may denote the joy that sparkled in his eyes or his comeliness; the witnesses note the eyes red with wine and the teeth white with milk — the closing figure of Judah's abundance of wine and milk, with a Messianic glance (Jacob completing the picture of plenty: 'His eyes shall be red with wine, and his teeth white with milk'; the meaning, not the 'intemperance of this tribe' but 'the goodness and generosity of their wine,' that Judah's land should so abound in wine and milk that the people should be ruddy and well-favoured, the eyes bright with the richness of wine and the teeth white with milk, a picture of health, plenty, and prosperity; that wine and milk together figure the fulness of the land, the vineyards and the pastures both yielding abundantly; the Messianic application the commentators offer, that as applied to Christ, the antitype of Judah, the figures may denote his comeliness, or the joy and the saving fulness found in him, who gives the wine and milk of the gospel freely; the richness of blessing that closes Judah's portion).

themes: His eyes shall be red with wine
and his teeth white with milk
the closing figure of Judah's abundance of wine and milk
a Messianic glance at the saving fulness in Christ, freely given

schools.summary: Gill: Zebulun should dwell at the haven of the sea, of the sea of Galilee and of the Mediterranean, his border toward the sea, fulfilled when settled in Canaan; the witnesses note Jacob foretelling that Zebulun should dwell at the haven of the sea, be a haven for ships, and his border reach to Zidon — the prophecy of Zebulun's sea-coast portion and his trade by ships (Jacob marking Zebulun's place by the sea: 'Zebulun shall dwell at the haven of the sea; and he shall be for an haven of ships; and his border shall be unto Zidon'; the foretelling of the lot, that Zebulun should 'dwell at the haven of the sea,' his inheritance lying toward the sea of Galilee and the Mediterranean both, so placed for harbours and traffic; the calling, 'he shall be for an haven of ships,' that his coast should be a refuge and station for shipping, the tribe given to trade and commerce by sea; the bound, 'his border shall be unto Zidon,' that his territory should reach toward the famous trading city of Zidon, sharing in its merchandise; the exactness of the prophecy, given long before Joshua divided the land yet fulfilled in Zebulun's allotted portion, a proof of the prophetic Spirit in Jacob's words).

themes: Zebulun shall dwell at the haven of the sea; and he shall be for an haven of ships
and his border shall be unto Zidon
the prophecy of Zebulun's sea-coast portion and trade by ships
the exactness of the prophecy, fulfilled long after, a proof of the prophetic Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: Issachar is compared to an ass, not for stupidity but a strong and robust one, able to carry burdens, a 'bony' ass, strong to labour; the witnesses note Jacob likening Issachar to a strong ass couching down between two burdens — the figure of a tribe strong but content to bear burdens for the sake of ease (Jacob marking Issachar's strength and his servile contentment: 'Issachar is a strong ass couching down between two burdens'; the figure, 'a strong ass,' not a reproach for stupidity but for strength, 'a bony one,' robust and able, strong to labour and to bear; the posture, 'couching down between two burdens,' that the strong ass lies down between the burdens or the sheepfolds, content to crouch under its load rather than fight for freedom; what this foretold of the tribe, that Issachar should be a strong and industrious people, given to husbandry and labour, but so loving ease and their pleasant land that they would rather bear burdens and pay tribute than rouse themselves to war for liberty; the mingled praise and reproof in the figure, strength joined to a love of ease).

themes: Issachar is a strong ass
couching down between two burdens
Issachar the strong but burden-bearing ass
a strong tribe that loved ease so well it would bear burdens rather than fight for liberty

schools.summary: Gill: he saw his part and portion of the good land allotted him, that it was pleasant, and chose rather to bear burdens and pay tribute than disturb his ease; the witnesses note Issachar seeing that rest was good and the land pleasant, and bowing his shoulder to bear and becoming a servant to tribute — the explanation of the figure, the tribe choosing ease and tribute above the cost of liberty (Jacob showing why Issachar bore the burden: 'he saw that rest was good, and the land that it was pleasant; and bowed his shoulder to bear, and became a servant unto tribute'; the reasoning, 'he saw that rest was good, and the land that it was pleasant,' that Issachar, finding his portion a fertile and pleasant land and loving his ease, judged quiet and rest better than the toil and danger of war; the choice, 'bowed his shoulder to bear, and became a servant unto tribute,' that, rather than rouse himself to defend his liberty, he submitted to bear the burden and pay tribute to keep his ease, choosing servitude with quiet over freedom with strife; the lesson the commentators draw, the danger of so loving ease and earthly comfort that one will purchase quiet even at the price of bondage; the mingled character of the tribe, strong yet servile by choice).

themes: he saw that rest was good, and the land that it was pleasant; and bowed his shoulder to bear
and became a servant unto tribute
Issachar choosing ease and tribute over the cost of liberty
the danger of loving ease so well one purchases quiet at the price of bondage

schools.summary: Gill: there is an elegant allusion to the name of Dan, which signifies to judge; the sense is there should be rulers and judges of it, as the other tribes had, and this is mentioned of him because he was a son of the handmaid; the witnesses note Jacob foretelling that Dan should judge his people as one of the tribes of Israel — the promise that Dan, though a son of the handmaid, should have his own rule and place among the tribes (Jacob honouring Dan with a word that plays on his name: 'Dan shall judge his people, as one of the tribes of Israel'; the play on the name, that 'Dan' means 'to judge,' so that 'Dan shall judge his people' fitly answers to his name; the sense, that Dan should have his own rulers and judges and a standing 'as one of the tribes of Israel,' full and equal with the rest, though he was the son of a handmaid, Bilhah; that this was a word of honour, that the lowlier-born son should yet be a true tribe with its own government; that the commentators see here a special pointing to Samson, the judge who arose from Dan and judged Israel, delivering them from the Philistines; the equal place of Dan among the brethren, none cast off).

themes: Dan shall judge his people
as one of the tribes of Israel
Dan given his own rule and place, full and equal though son of the handmaid
the commentators seeing a pointing to Samson, the judge who arose from Dan

schools.summary: Gill: Dan should be like the adder or cerastes, which lies among the sand, of the same colour, not easily discerned, and bites at once, unexpected; the witnesses note Jacob likening Dan to a serpent by the way, an adder in the path, biting the horse's heels so the rider falls — the figure of Dan's subtle and sudden craft, overcoming the stronger by stratagem (Jacob marking Dan's way of warfare: 'Dan shall be a serpent by the way, an adder in the path, that biteth the horse heels, so that his rider shall fall backward'; the figure of the serpent, 'a serpent by the way, an adder in the path,' likely the cerastes, which lies hidden in the sand of like colour, unseen, and strikes suddenly; the manner of conquest, 'that biteth the horse heels, so that his rider shall fall backward,' that Dan, though a smaller tribe, should overcome stronger foes not by open force but by subtlety and ambush, as the small serpent fells the great horse and rider by a sudden bite; the commentators' application to Samson, the Danite, who by craft and sudden surprise destroyed many of the Philistines, the strong overthrown by the cunning of the weak; the mingled character, that subtlety may be a weapon for good against the enemy, yet the serpent figure also hints at Dan's later snares).

themes: Dan shall be a serpent by the way, an adder in the path
that biteth the horse heels, so that his rider shall fall backward
Dan overcoming stronger foes by subtlety and ambush
applied to Samson, the strong overthrown by the cunning of the weak

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob, finding his spirits faint and flag, stops and breathes awhile, and as a dying man expresses what he had been thoughtful of, waiting for God's salvation; the witnesses note Jacob, amid the prophecies, breaking out, 'I have waited for thy salvation, O LORD' — the dying patriarch's sudden ejaculation of faith, looking beyond all the tribes to God's salvation (Jacob lifting his heart to the LORD: 'I have waited for thy salvation, O LORD'; the occasion, that Jacob, his spirits flagging in the long prophecy and feeling himself a dying man, pauses to breathe and to lift up his soul to God; the substance of the cry, 'I have waited for thy salvation,' that, looking back over his life and forward beyond death, Jacob professes that all his hope had been and still was in 'thy salvation, O LORD,' the deliverance God should send; the deeper reach the commentators find, that Jacob waited not merely for temporal deliverances but for the great salvation of God, the coming of the promised Saviour, the Shiloh of whom he had just spoken; the beauty of a dying saint's faith, resting not on his sons' fortunes but on the LORD's salvation; the LORD named here, the covenant God in whom Jacob trusted).

debates.question: What salvation does Jacob wait for?

sides.position: Not temporal deliverance only, but the great salvation of God — the coming of the promised Saviour, the Shiloh of whom he had just spoken (v.10); the dying patriarch's faith resting on the LORD's salvation

themes: I have waited for thy salvation
O LORD
the dying patriarch's sudden cry of faith amid the prophecies
waiting not for temporal deliverance only but for the promised Saviour, the Shiloh

schools.summary: Gill: there is a paronomasia, an allusion to the name of Gad almost in every word, which signifies a troop; the prediction is that this tribe would be warlike, sometimes conquered but conquering at last; the witnesses note Jacob foretelling that a troop should overcome Gad but he should overcome at the last — the prophecy of a warlike tribe, conquered for a time yet victorious in the end (Jacob marking Gad's wars with a play on his name: 'Gad, a troop shall overcome him: but he shall overcome at the last'; the play on the name, that 'Gad' means 'a troop,' and the verse rings with the word; the meaning, that Gad's tribe, lying exposed beyond Jordan, should be a warlike people, much beset and sometimes overcome by raiding troops of enemies, the Ammonites and others; the comfort in the close, 'but he shall overcome at the last,' that, though pressed and beaten for a season, Gad should rally and conquer in the end, the final victory his; the lesson the commentators draw for the believer, that the people of God may be overcome for a time yet shall overcome at the last, the final triumph sure).

themes: Gad, a troop shall overcome him
but he shall overcome at the last
Gad a warlike tribe, conquered for a time yet victorious in the end
a lesson that the people of God may be overcome for a season yet triumph at last

schools.summary: Gill: this signifies that this tribe would have a sufficiency of food out of their own land, of the best sort, occupying a rich tract reaching from Zidon to Carmel; the witnesses note Jacob foretelling that Asher's bread should be fat and he should yield royal dainties — the prophecy of a rich and fertile portion, fit to furnish the tables of kings (Jacob marking Asher's fatness and plenty: 'Out of Asher his bread shall be fat, and he shall yield royal dainties'; the figure of plenty, 'his bread shall be fat,' that Asher should have abundance of food out of his own land, and that of the best and richest sort, the soil so fertile that the tribe should want nothing; the excellence of the produce, 'he shall yield royal dainties,' that Asher's land should bring forth such choice fruits, oil, and provisions as were fit for a king, dainties to furnish royal tables; that Asher's portion, the rich coastland from Zidon toward Carmel, was indeed famous for its fat soil, its oil and corn; the exact answering of the prophecy to the inheritance).

themes: Out of Asher his bread shall be fat
and he shall yield royal dainties
Asher's rich and fertile portion, fat bread and royal dainties
abundance of the best, fit for kings, answering to the rich coastland

schools.summary: Gill: Onkelos applies it to the tribe and the goodness of its land, his lot falling in a good and fruit-bearing land, the fruitful country of Gennesaret; the witnesses note Jacob likening Naphtali to a hind let loose, that giveth goodly words — the figure of a free and swift tribe, fruitful in eloquence and good tidings (Jacob marking Naphtali's freedom and his goodly speech: 'Naphtali is a hind let loose: he giveth goodly words'; the figure of the 'hind let loose,' a deer set free, swift, graceful, and at liberty, that bounds over the hills, some applying it to the tribe's freedom and agility, others, with Onkelos, to the goodness of its land, the fruitful country of Gennesaret where Naphtali's lot fell; the second clause, 'he giveth goodly words,' that Naphtali should be a people fruitful in fair and pleasant speech, eloquent, bearing good tidings, or yielding the goodly fruits of a fertile land; the commentators' note that from the borders of Naphtali, by the sea of Galilee, came much of the preaching of Christ and his apostles, the goodly words of the gospel; the gracious character of the tribe, free and fruitful in good words).

themes: Naphtali is a hind let loose
he giveth goodly words
Naphtali the free and swift hind, fruitful in fair speech
the commentators noting that from his borders by Galilee came the gospel preaching of Christ

schools.summary: Gill: Joseph is like a bough or branch laden with fruit, as he was with children, both his sons becoming numerous and heads of tribes, and with other blessings; the witnesses note Jacob likening Joseph to a fruitful bough by a well, whose branches run over the wall — the figure of Joseph's fruitfulness, spreading and abundant, watered by God (Jacob beginning Joseph's rich blessing with the image of fruitfulness: 'Joseph is a fruitful bough, even a fruitful bough by a well; whose branches run over the wall'; the figure of the 'fruitful bough,' a branch laden with fruit, fitly figuring Joseph, fruitful in children, Ephraim named for fruitfulness and Manasseh, both heads of tribes, and in every blessing; the source of the fruitfulness, 'by a well,' that the bough is planted by a spring of water, ever supplied and never failing, drawing from the unfailing well of God's grace; the spreading, 'whose branches run over the wall,' that Joseph's fruitfulness should not be confined but overflow, his descendants multiplying and spreading abroad beyond all bounds; the type the commentators draw, of Christ and his church, fruitful by the well of living water and spreading over every wall to the Gentiles).

themes: Joseph is a fruitful bough, even a fruitful bough by a well
whose branches run over the wall
Joseph the fruitful bough, watered by the unfailing well of God's grace
spreading beyond all bounds, a type of Christ and his church

schools.summary: Gill: his brethren grieved him with their ill usage, shot out bitter words against him, hated him for his dreams and because his father loved him, and could not speak peaceably to him; the witnesses note Jacob recalling how the archers sorely grieved Joseph, shot at him, and hated him — the looking back on Joseph's sufferings at the hands of his enemies, chiefly his own brethren (Jacob remembering the affliction Joseph bore: 'The archers have sorely grieved him, and shot at him, and hated him'; who the 'archers' were, chiefly his brethren, who 'grieved him with their ill usage,' 'shot out bitter words against him' like arrows, and 'hated him' for his dreams and for his father's love, and could not speak peaceably to him; the further enemies, Potiphar's wife and the like, who also shot at his integrity; the sharpness of the trial, that Joseph was the mark of envy, slander, and hatred, sorely wounded by those nearest him; the type of Christ the commentators draw, that Joseph, hated and assailed by his own brethren without cause, foreshadows the Saviour, hated and shot at by his own; that the recital of the sufferings sets off the deliverance that follows).

debates.question: Whom does the hated and assailed Joseph foreshadow?

sides.position: Christ — Joseph, grieved, shot at, and hated by his own brethren without cause, foreshadows the Saviour hated and assailed by his own (cf. John 1:11)

themes: The archers have sorely grieved him, and shot at him
and hated him
Joseph grieved and hated by the archers, chiefly his brethren
a type of Christ, hated and assailed by his own without cause

schools.summary: Gill: his bow abode in strength; as his enemies were archers so had he a bow, but of a different sort, the virtues and graces he possessed, his strength being from God; the arms of his hands were made strong by the mighty God of Jacob; the witnesses note Joseph's bow abiding in strength, his arms made strong by the hands of the mighty God of Jacob, from whom is the shepherd, the stone of Israel — the divine upholding of Joseph against his foes, and the great titles of God (Jacob showing whence Joseph's strength came: 'his bow abode in strength, and the arms of his hands were made strong by the hands of the mighty God of Jacob; from thence is the shepherd, the stone of Israel'; the victory, 'his bow abode in strength,' that, assailed by the archers, Joseph yet stood firm, his strength holding, repelling their force not by their kind of weapons but by his innocence, integrity, and faith; the true source, 'the arms of his hands were made strong by the hands of the mighty God of Jacob,' that it was God, the mighty God of Jacob, who upheld and strengthened him through all his trials; the great titles, 'the shepherd, the stone of Israel,' that from this same God come the Shepherd who feeds and the Stone who upholds his people, titles the commentators apply to Christ; that Joseph's whole preservation and rise were of God's strengthening).

outliers: the shepherd, the stone of Israel — titles applied by the commentators to Christ, the Shepherd and the Stone
themes: his bow abode in strength, and the arms of his hands were made strong by the hands of the mighty God of Jacob
from thence is the shepherd, the stone of Israel
God's upholding of Joseph against his foes by his own strength
the titles 'the shepherd, the stone of Israel' applied to Christ

schools.summary: Gill: this is the mighty God of Jacob, who had made his hands strong and would still help, protect, and defend him, and by whom Christ the antitype was helped; the witnesses note Jacob blessing Joseph by the God of his father and the Almighty, with blessings of heaven, of the deep, of the breasts and the womb — the heaping up of every kind of blessing upon Joseph from the all-sufficient God (Jacob pouring out a full and many-sided blessing: 'by the God of thy father, who shall help thee; and by the Almighty, who shall bless thee with blessings of heaven above, blessings of the deep that lieth under, blessings of the breasts, and of the womb'; the source, 'the God of thy father, who shall help thee... the Almighty, who shall bless thee,' that all Joseph's blessing flows from the covenant God of Jacob, the 'Almighty,' El-Shaddai, all-sufficient to help and to bless; the fulness, 'blessings of heaven above,' the rain, sun, and dew, 'blessings of the deep that lieth under,' the springs and waters beneath, and 'blessings of the breasts, and of the womb,' fruitfulness of family and flock, every kind of good from heaven, earth, and the increase of living things; that the all-sufficient God would supply Joseph from every quarter, above and below; the bounty of grace, that God blesses his own with fulness).

themes: by the God of thy father, who shall help thee; and by the Almighty, who shall bless thee
blessings of heaven above, blessings of the deep that lieth under, blessings of the breasts, and of the womb
every kind of blessing heaped on Joseph from the all-sufficient God
blessings of heaven, deep, and increase, the bounty of grace

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob's blessings were greater and more numerous than those Abraham and Isaac had, and should be on the head of Joseph, who was separate from his brethren; the witnesses note Jacob declaring his blessings on Joseph prevailed above those of his progenitors, to the utmost bound of the everlasting hills, to be on the head of Joseph, separate from his brethren — the crowning of the blessing, the abundance set on Joseph's head (Jacob capping the blessing with its surpassing greatness: 'the blessings of thy father have prevailed above the blessings of my progenitors unto the utmost bound of the everlasting hills: they shall be on the head of Joseph'; the greatness, 'have prevailed above the blessings of my progenitors,' that the blessings Jacob bestowed, by the Spirit, exceeded those Abraham and Isaac had given, more full and numerous; the reach, 'unto the utmost bound of the everlasting hills,' that the blessings should extend as far and last as long as the perpetual hills, abundant and enduring; the resting-place, 'on the head of Joseph, and on the crown of the head of him that was separate from his brethren,' that all this blessing should crown Joseph, who 'was separate from his brethren,' set apart by his sufferings and his exaltation, a fit type of Christ, the Nazarite separated from sinners; the honour put on the once-rejected son).

themes: the blessings of thy father have prevailed above the blessings of my progenitors unto the utmost bound of the everlasting hills
on the crown of the head of him that was separate from his brethren
Jacob's blessings on Joseph surpassing those of his fathers, abundant and enduring
crowning the head of Joseph, separated from his brethren, a type of Christ

schools.summary: Gill: the Targums apply this to the priests offering the daily sacrifice in the temple, which stood in Benjamin's lot, but it chiefly respects the warlike and victorious character of the tribe; the witnesses note Jacob likening Benjamin to a wolf that ravins, devouring the prey in the morning and dividing the spoil at night — the figure of a fierce and warlike tribe, victorious in battle (Jacob closing the sons' blessings with Benjamin the wolf: 'Benjamin shall ravin as a wolf: in the morning he shall devour the prey, and at night he shall divide the spoil'; the figure of the 'wolf,' fierce, swift, and ravening, fitly figuring Benjamin's warlike spirit, the tribe famous for valiant and skilful warriors, the slingers and the men of Gibeah, and Saul and Jonathan; the picture of constant victory, 'in the morning he shall devour the prey, and at night he shall divide the spoil,' that the tribe should be successful in war from morning to night, seizing the prey and sharing out the spoil, a conqueror all the day; the further application some draw, to Saul the Benjamite, the first king, and to Paul the Benjamite, who as a wolf first ravined against the church and then, transformed, divided the spoil of the gospel; the fierce and victorious character that closes the tribal blessings).

themes: Benjamin shall ravin as a wolf: in the morning he shall devour the prey
and at night he shall divide the spoil
Benjamin the ravening, victorious wolf, a fierce and warlike tribe
some applying it to Saul the first king and to Paul, who divided the spoil of the gospel

schools.summary: Gill: the twelve sons were heads of twelve tribes, afterwards seated in Canaan, though there were thirteen tribes, two springing from Joseph; the witnesses note the summary that all these are the twelve tribes of Israel, and that their father blessed every one according to his own blessing — the gathering-up of the whole prophecy, each son blessed with his fitting word (the narrator summing the blessing: 'All these are the twelve tribes of Israel... and blessed them; every one according to his blessing he blessed them'; the summing, 'All these are the twelve tribes of Israel,' that the twelve sons just addressed were the heads of the twelve tribes, the foundations of the nation, afterward seated in Canaan; the manner of the blessing, 'every one according to his blessing he blessed them,' that Jacob gave to each son the word that fitted him, suited to his character and his future, whether of honour or of reproof, of plenty or of war; that even the words of sentence, to Reuben, Simeon, and Levi, are reckoned within 'blessed them,' for the prophecy as a whole was a blessing to the house, ordering each in his place; the wisdom and impartiality of the dying father, dealing with each according to truth).

themes: All these are the twelve tribes of Israel
every one according to his blessing he blessed them
the summing-up, each of the twelve blessed with his fitting word
even the words of sentence reckoned within the blessing to the house

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Jacob charged them, saying, I am to be gathered unto my people, bury me with my fathers in the cave in the field of Ephron the Hittite; Gill: the same charge he had given to Joseph he here renews and lays upon all his sons, who were to go along with Joseph to bury him in Canaan; the witnesses note Jacob charging his sons that, being gathered to his people, they should bury him with his fathers in the cave of Ephron the Hittite — the dying patriarch's solemn charge to be buried in the land of promise (Jacob, the blessing ended, giving his last command: 'I am to be gathered unto my people: bury me with my fathers in the cave that is in the field of Ephron the Hittite'; the phrase 'I am to be gathered unto my people,' the language of faith, that death was to him a gathering to his fathers and to the people of God, not a perishing; the charge, 'bury me with my fathers... in the field of Ephron the Hittite,' that he renews to all his sons the charge given to Joseph, that he be carried out of Egypt and buried in the ancestral tomb in Canaan; the faith in the charge, that Jacob would lie not in Egypt but in the land of promise, professing his hope in the covenant and the inheritance God had sworn; that the burial in Canaan was a testimony that Egypt was not their home but the promised land).

themes: I am to be gathered unto my people
bury me with my fathers in the cave that is in the field of Ephron the Hittite
Jacob's dying charge to be buried with his fathers in Canaan
the language of faith, his burial in the land of promise a testimony of his covenant hope

schools.summary: Gill: this is so exactly described that there might be no mistake about the place, the cave in the field of Machpelah before Mamre, in the land of Canaan, which Abraham bought for a possession of a burying-place; the witnesses note Jacob exactly naming the cave of Machpelah before Mamre, which Abraham bought for a burying-place — the careful pointing to the ancestral tomb, the bought possession in the land of promise (Jacob marking the very place with care: 'the cave that is in the field of Machpelah, which is before Mamre, in the land of Canaan, which Abraham bought... for a possession of a buryingplace'; the exactness, that Jacob describes the place so fully that there might be no mistake about the place, his sons knowing exactly where to carry him; the title to it, 'which Abraham bought with the field of Ephron the Hittite for a possession of a buryingplace,' that this was the lawful, purchased possession of the family, the one piece of the promised land they then held, bought by Abraham; the faith in choosing this tomb, that Jacob would lie in the bought ground of Canaan, a pledge of the whole land promised to his seed; the continuity of the covenant family, laid together in one tomb).

themes: the cave that is in the field of Machpelah, which is before Mamre, in the land of Canaan
which Abraham bought with the field of Ephron the Hittite for a possession of a buryingplace
Jacob exactly naming the ancestral tomb, Abraham's bought possession
a pledge of the whole land promised to his seed

schools.summary: Gill: Abraham buried Sarah there himself, and his sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him there, and Jacob there buried Leah; the witnesses note Jacob recounting that there were buried Abraham and Sarah, Isaac and Rebekah, and Leah — the roll of the fathers laid in the one tomb, into whose company Jacob would be gathered (Jacob naming the patriarchs and matriarchs of the family tomb: 'There they buried Abraham and Sarah his wife; there they buried Isaac and Rebekah his wife; and there I buried Leah'; the three couples named, 'Abraham and Sarah,' 'Isaac and Rebekah,' and Leah, Jacob's wife buried by him there, the patriarchs and their wives, the covenant fathers and mothers, all laid together in Machpelah; the honour Jacob does to Leah, 'there I buried Leah,' owning her in death as his wife and laying her among the patriarchs, though Rachel had been the beloved; the desire of Jacob to be gathered into this honoured company, to lie with the fathers of the covenant in the bought ground of promise; the unity of the covenant family in life and death).

themes: There they buried Abraham and Sarah his wife; there they buried Isaac and Rebekah his wife
and there I buried Leah
Jacob naming the covenant fathers and mothers laid in Machpelah
his desire to be gathered into their honoured company in the land of promise

schools.summary: Gill: this is repeated for the certainty of it, that both field and cave were bought by Abraham of Ephron the Hittite, that it might be taken notice of; the witnesses note Jacob noting again that the field and cave were purchased from the children of Heth — the careful repeating of the lawful title, that the burial ground was a sure and bought possession (Jacob sealing the matter of the title: 'The purchase of the field and of the cave that is therein was from the children of Heth'; the repetition, that this is said again 'for the certainty of it,' that there might be no doubt that 'both the field and cave were bought by Abraham of Ephron the Hittite,' lawfully and fully purchased; the importance the commentators mark, that the family had a clear and undisputed title to this one piece of Canaan, a sure possession 'from the children of Heth,' the men of the land; the faith and foresight in this, that Abraham had secured, and Jacob now confirmed, a holding in the promised land, an earnest of the whole inheritance to come; the care of the patriarchs to keep a sure footing in the land of promise).

themes: The purchase of the field and of the cave that is therein
was from the children of Heth
the repeated confirming of the lawful, bought title to the tomb
the family's one clear holding in Canaan, an earnest of the whole inheritance

schools.summary: Gill: when Jacob had given all the proper directions concerning his interment, he gathered up his feet into the bed on which he sat while he blessed his sons; the witnesses note Jacob, having ended commanding his sons, gathering up his feet into the bed, yielding up the ghost, and being gathered to his people — the calm and faithful death of the patriarch, his work finished and his spirit going home (Jacob dying in peace, his task complete: 'when Jacob had made an end of commanding his sons, he gathered up his feet into the bed, and yielded up the ghost, and was gathered unto his people'; the order, 'when Jacob had made an end of commanding his sons,' that he died with his work done, his blessing given and his charge laid, nothing left undone; the calm, 'he gathered up his feet into the bed,' that, having sat up to bless his sons, he composedly drew up his feet and lay down, as one ready and willing to die; the gentle passing, 'and yielded up the ghost,' that he gave up his spirit peacefully into the hand of God, no struggle, the death of the righteous; the comfort, 'and was gathered unto his people,' that, as his soul departed, he was gathered to the company of the faithful gone before, the language of immortality and of faith; the blessed end of a long pilgrimage, the patriarch entering into rest).

debates.question: What does 'gathered unto his people' signify?

sides.position: The language of immortality and faith — that as his soul departed, Jacob was gathered to the company of the faithful gone before; not mere burial but the going home of the spirit, the death of the righteous entering into rest

themes: he gathered up his feet into the bed, and yielded up the ghost
and was gathered unto his people
Jacob's calm and faithful death, his work done
the death of the righteous, the language of immortality and faith, entering into rest
